intro.hero_title: Simulador World3 — O que acontece com a civilização até 2100?
intro.body_1_html: Em 1972, uma equipe de pesquisadores do MIT construiu o World3 — um modelo computacional que simula como população, produção industrial, produção de alimentos, poluição e recursos naturais interagem ao longo do tempo. Suas conclusões, publicadas em Limites do Crescimento (The Limits to Growth), mostraram que o cenário tendencial leva ao overshoot e ao declínio.
intro.body_2_html: Esta ferramenta permite que você explore esses cenários. Escolha um cenário predefinido para ver o que acontece sob diferentes suposições sobre tecnologia, políticas e recursos — ou ajuste parâmetros individuais no editor avançado.

model.title: Como funciona o modelo World3?
model.hero_body_html: Em 1972, pesquisadores construíram um modelo computacional da trajetória da civilização. Ele rastreia cinco coisas: pessoas, produção industrial, alimentos, poluição e recursos naturais. Abaixo, percorremos as grandes perguntas que ele responde — e mostramos exatamente o que ele assume.
model.references.items.0: Meadows, D. H., Meadows, D. L., Randers, J. & Behrens, W. W. — Limites do Crescimento (The Limits to Growth) (1972)
model.references.items.1: Meadows, D. L., Behrens, W. W., Meadows, D. H., Naill, R. F., Randers, J. & Zahn, E. — Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World) (1974)
model.references.items.2: Meadows, D. H., Randers, J. & Meadows, D. L. — Limites do Crescimento: Atualização de 30 anos (Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update) (2005)
model.references.items.4: Nebel, A., Kling, A., Willamowski, R. & Schell, T. — recalibração do PyWorld3-03 (2024)
model.references.items.5: Our World in Data — dados de validação
model.assumptions_desc: Cada constante abaixo é uma premissa inicial do modelo World3. Estão agrupadas por setor. Você pode editar qualquer uma no editor avançado.
model.section.pop-capacity.question: A Terra pode sustentar 10 bilhões de pessoas?
model.section.pop-capacity.summary: O modelo diz que depende da velocidade com que consumimos recursos e de quanta poluição geramos. A população atinge um pico e depois declina quando os alimentos ou serviços de saúde não conseguem mais acompanhar. No cenário padrão, a população atinge o pico por volta de 2030, com cerca de 8,4 bilhões, e depois cai conforme a expectativa de vida diminui.
model.section.pop-capacity.concepts.0.de: O World3 divide a população em quatro grupos: 0–14, 15–44, 45–64 e 65+. As pessoas passam de uma coorte para a seguinte conforme envelhecem. Cada coorte tem sua própria taxa de mortalidade, influenciada por alimentação, poluição, serviços de saúde e aglomeração.
model.section.pop-capacity.concepts.1.de: A fecundidade depende do tamanho desejado da família, que por sua vez depende da renda e da expectativa de vida. Conforme a produção industrial aumenta, as famílias escolhem ter menos filhos. Um fator de "eficácia do controle de fecundidade" (0–1) modela quão bem a contracepção funciona, melhorando ao longo do tempo com os serviços de saúde.
model.section.pop-capacity.equations.pre: A população é a soma de quatro estoques de coortes (P1–P4). Cada estoque muda por entradas de nascimentos ou maturação e saídas por morte ou passagem para o próximo grupo. A expectativa de vida é um valor base multiplicado por quatro fatores.
model.section.pop-capacity.equations.fee: Este é um loop de reforço: maior expectativa de vida → mais pessoas → mais produção industrial → melhores serviços de saúde → maior expectativa de vida. Mas a poluição e a escassez de alimentos criam loops de equilíbrio que acabam dominando.
model.section.pop-capacity.sources.0.tex: Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World), cap. 2 — Setor de população
model.section.pop-capacity.sources.1.tex: Limites do Crescimento: Atualização de 30 anos (Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update), cap. 3
model.section.resource-depletion.summary: No cenário padrão do modelo, os recursos não renováveis (combustíveis fósseis, metais, minerais) diminuem constantemente e estão cerca de 50% esgotados nos anos 2020. Conforme ficam escassos, cada vez mais capital é desviado da indústria para a extração, arrastando toda a economia. O modelo não diz que literalmente "acabam" — diz que a extração fica tão cara que sufoca o crescimento.
model.section.resource-depletion.concept: Os recursos não renováveis no World3 são um estoque agregado representando combustíveis fósseis (carvão, petróleo, gás), minerais e metais. Renováveis como solar, eólica e hidrelétrica NÃO estão incluídos — o modelo foi construído antes de os renováveis serem significativos. Essa é uma de suas limitações conhecidas.
Fração do capital alocada para obtenção de recursos (FCAOR)

Conforme os recursos se esgotam, a economia precisa gastar mais capital em extração (minas mais profundas, depósitos mais difíceis de acessar). O FCAOR sobe de cerca de 5% para mais de 50%, deixando menos capital para indústria, agricultura e serviços. Este é o mecanismo-chave que desencadeia o declínio.
model.section.resource-depletion.equatio: Os recursos são um estoque único que se esgota a uma taxa proporcional à população e ao consumo per capita. Um multiplicador tecnológico pode desacelerar o esgotamento.
Este é um loop de equilíbrio: menos recursos → FCAOR mais alto → menos produção industrial → menor taxa de consumo → esgotamento mais lento. Mas a economia encolhe no processo.
model.section.resource-depletion.sources: Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World), cap. 5 — Setor de recursos não renováveis
model.section.technology-rescue.question: A tecnologia pode nos salvar?
model.section.technology-rescue.summary: O modelo testa isso comparando o cenário padrão com um cenário otimista onde a tecnologia melhora a eficiência de recursos, o controle de poluição e os rendimentos agrícolas a partir de 1975. A tecnologia ajuda — adia o pico e eleva o teto — mas na maioria dos cenários não evita o overshoot sozinha. O crescimento do consumo supera os ganhos.
model.section.technology-rescue.concepts: Como a tecnologia entra no modelo

A tecnologia é modelada por constantes de "chaveamento" que alternam entre duas funções tabuladas em um determinado ano. Por exemplo, NRUF muda da tabela 1 para a tabela 2, representando uma melhoria repentina na eficiência de uso de recursos. Isso é reconhecidamente simplista — a adoção tecnológica real é gradual.

Constantes como LFERT, NRUF, PPOLX têm dois conjuntos de tabelas de consulta. Um ano de chaveamento (ex.: pyear_policy_switch = 1975 em cenários otimistas) determina quando o modelo transita das tabelas "tendenciais" para as tabelas "melhoradas por tecnologia".
model.section.technology-rescue.equation: A tecnologia atua por meio de funções multiplicadoras que modificam o uso de recursos, a geração de poluição e o rendimento da terra. O ano de chaveamento determina quando as melhorias começam.
Fator de uso de recursos (após chaveamento)

A tecnologia cria um loop de equilíbrio contra o esgotamento e a poluição, mas o crescimento da população e do consumo cria loops de reforço que podem superar os ganhos. O modelo sugere que a tecnologia é necessária, mas insuficiente sem mudanças do lado da demanda.
Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World), cap. 7 — Cenários de política
Limites do Crescimento: Atualização de 30 anos (Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update), cap. 7 — Tecnologia
model.section.pollution-cost.question: Qual é o real custo da poluição?
model.section.pollution-cost.summary: No cenário padrão, o índice de poluição permanece baixo até cerca de 2000, depois sobe exponencialmente quando a produção industrial atinge o pico. A poluição reduz a fertilidade do solo (menos alimentos) e a expectativa de vida (mais mortes). Quando a poluição atinge o pico, o dano já está consolidado porque poluentes persistentes levam décadas para serem absorvidos.
Poluentes de longa duração que se acumulam no meio ambiente — metais pesados, produtos químicos sintéticos, plásticos e gases de efeito estufa. O modelo usa um índice agregado único em vez de rastrear cada substância individualmente.
O World3 foi construído em 1972, antes de a ciência do clima ser estabelecida. Ele modela a poluição como um estoque genérico que afeta saúde e agricultura. Na validação, a concentração de CO₂ serve como proxy empírico para PPOLX, de modo que os efeitos climáticos são capturados indiretamente pelo índice de poluição.
O meio ambiente absorve a poluição ao longo do tempo, mas há um atraso (AHL70 ≈ 1,5 ano nos níveis de 1970). Conforme a poluição aumenta, a meia-vida de absorção pode crescer, significando que o ambiente perde capacidade de se limpar — um feedback positivo perigoso.
model.section.pollution-cost.equations.p: A poluição persistente se acumula a partir da atividade industrial e agrícola e é lentamente absorvida pelo meio ambiente. O índice de poluição (PPOLX) é a razão entre a poluição atual e seu nível de referência de 1970.
model.section.pollution-cost.equations.f: Poluição crescente → menor fertilidade do solo → menos alimentos → maior mortalidade → menor população → menos poluição (equilíbrio). Mas também: poluição crescente → maior tempo de absorção → ainda mais poluição (reforço). O loop de reforço domina durante a fase de overshoot.
model.section.pollution-cost.sources.0.t: Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World), cap. 4 — Setor de poluição
model.section.pollution-cost.sources.1.t: Nebel et al. (2024) — CO₂ como proxy de validação para PPOL
model.section.food-enough.question: Há comida suficiente?
model.section.food-enough.summary: No cenário padrão, os alimentos per capita aumentam até cerca de 2010 e depois diminuem conforme a terra arável se erode, a poluição reduz a fertilidade e o capital é desviado para a extração de recursos. O modelo não prevê fome para todos — mostra que a média global cai abaixo do nível necessário para manter a expectativa de vida.
model.section.food-enough.concepts.0.def: Terra adequada para cultivo. No World3, a terra arável pode ser desenvolvida (convertida de outros usos) ou perdida por erosão e expansão urbana. Uma vez perdida, leva muito tempo para se recuperar. O estoque inicial é de cerca de 0,9 bilhão de hectares.
model.section.food-enough.concepts.1.def: Quanta comida é produzida por hectare. Depende dos insumos agrícolas (capital, fertilizantes) e da fertilidade do solo. A poluição degrada a fertilidade do solo ao longo do tempo. A tecnologia pode aumentar o rendimento, mas com retornos decrescentes.
model.section.food-enough.equations.prea: A produção de alimentos é o rendimento da terra vezes a terra arável, dividido pela população para o valor per capita. O rendimento depende dos insumos agrícolas e da fertilidade.
model.section.food-enough.equations.feed: Mais capital agrícola → maior rendimento → mais alimentos → menor mortalidade → mais pessoas → mais capital necessário (reforço). Mas a poluição degrada a fertilidade e a terra arável se erode, criando loops de equilíbrio.
model.section.food-enough.sources.0.text: Dinâmica do crescimento em um mundo finito (Dynamics of Growth in a Finite World), cap. 3 — Setor agrícola
model.section.food-enough.sources.1.text: Limites do Crescimento: Atualização de 30 anos (Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update), cap. 4
explore.title: Explorar um cenário
explore.classic_help: Cinco indicadores-chave do World3 em um único gráfico.
compare.title: Comparar cenários
advanced.title: Avançado: editar constantes
advanced.subtitle: Ajuste qualquer constante do modelo World3 abaixo. A simulação é atualizada automaticamente quando você altera valores. Cada constante controla um aspecto específico do modelo.
calibrate.title: Calibrar e validar
calibrate.intro_html: Calibre constantes do modelo e valide a saída da simulação contra observações do mundo real de Our World in Data (OWID).
calibrate.section_title: Calibrar constantes a partir do OWID
calibrate.section_desc_html: Busca dados observados do Our World in Data para a entidade e ano de referência escolhidos, depois ajusta constantes do modelo World3 para corresponder às observações reais. A tabela abaixo mostra qual indicador OWID foi usado para cada constante e quão confiável é o mapeamento.
validate.section_desc_html: Executa uma simulação World3 padrão e compara cada variável de saída com a série temporal OWID correspondente. A tabela de métricas mostra o quão bem a simulação acompanha os dados observados (RMSE, MAPE, correlação) e qual indicador OWID é usado para cada variável.
errors.init_failed_title: Falha ao inicializar o aplicativo nativo do navegador.
errors.init_failed_body: Recarregue a página e verifique se os arquivos de dados estáticos estão disponíveis.

intro.ack.item_6_html: Built by serroba — @serroba
intro.ack.item_0_html: Clube de Roma — encomendou o estudo original Limites do Crescimento no MIT (1972)
history.title: A história por trás do modelo
history.subtitle: De um relatório controverso de 1972 ao quadro dos limites planetários — como Os Limites do Crescimento moldou nossa compreensão da sustentabilidade global.
history.origin.title: 1972: A World Model Is Born
history.origin.body_html: In 1968, Italian industrialist Aurelio Peccei and Scottish scientist Alexander King founded the Club of Rome, a think tank concerned with long-term global challenges. They commissioned a team at MIT — building on Jay Forrester’s pioneering World Dynamics model (1971) — led by Dennis Meadows and Donella Meadows to build a computer simulation of the world system. The result was World3, and its findings were published in 1972 as The Limits to Growth. The book sold over 30 million copies in 30 languages, making it one of the best-selling environmental books in history. Its central message: exponential growth in population and consumption on a finite planet leads to overshoot and collapse — unless society deliberately changes course.
history.backlash.title: A reação: "Eles erraram"
history.backlash.body_html: Almost immediately, economists and politicians pushed back. Julian Simon called the study “neo-Malthusian nonsense.” William Nordhaus published a critique in 1992 arguing the model was too simplistic. The most common misconception: that the book predicted the world would “run out of resources by 2000.” It never said that. The model explored scenarios — it showed that overshoot dynamics (delayed feedback, exponential growth hitting limits) would cause decline, not that any single resource would hit zero on a fixed date. By the 1990s, the conventional wisdom was that The Limits to Growth had been “debunked.” This narrative persisted for decades.
history.vindication.title: 2008–2014: Os dados alcançam a realidade
history.vindication.body_html: In 2008, physicist Graham Turner (paper) at Australia’s CSIRO compared 30 years of real-world data against the original 1972 scenarios. His finding: the “standard run” (business-as-usual) scenario tracked observed data with remarkable accuracy. Population, industrial output, food production, pollution, and resource depletion were all following the trajectory World3 had projected. Turner updated his analysis in 2014 with 40 years of data and the conclusion held. The “debunked” narrative began to unravel. The model had not predicted exact dates or precise numbers — it had captured the structural dynamics of overshoot, and those dynamics were playing out as modeled.
history.updates.title: 2005 e 2022: Revisitando os limites
history.updates.body_html: In 2005, the original authors published Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, concluding that humanity had already overshot several critical limits and that the window for a smooth, managed transition was narrowing. By 2022, on the 50th anniversary, Dennis Meadows reflected that the opportunity for a gradual correction had likely passed — the question was no longer whether overshoot would occur, but how societies would adapt to it. In a 2022 interview with Nate Hagens, Meadows reflected that the model was never meant to predict specific dates — it was meant to give people a conceptual framework for understanding how growth, feedback loops, and delays interact on a finite planet. He noted that the standard run’s projections of industrial output peaking around 2020 and population peaking around 2030 were tracking observed data remarkably well. In 2023, the Earth Commission’s Safe and Just Earth System Boundaries study confirmed that six of nine planetary boundaries had already been crossed.
history.boundaries.title: Limites planetários: O quadro moderno
history.boundaries.body_html: In 2009, Johan Rockström and a team of Earth system scientists introduced the Planetary Boundaries framework, identifying nine Earth system processes with thresholds that, if crossed, could trigger irreversible environmental change. The nine boundaries — climate change, biodiversity loss, nitrogen and phosphorus flows, ocean acidification, land-use change, freshwater use, ozone depletion, atmospheric aerosols, and chemical pollution — map directly onto the dynamics World3 modeled decades earlier. Where World3 used aggregate stocks (resources, pollution, food, population), Planetary Boundaries gives each limit a name and a measurement. The intellectual lineage is clear: both frameworks say that growth within a finite system encounters hard limits, and that crossing those limits triggers reinforcing feedback loops that are difficult to reverse. The Limits to Growth was the warning. Planetary Boundaries is the diagnosis.
intro.ack.item_7_html: Breaking Down: Collapse — Ep. 4: Overshoot & Limits to Growth (podcast, 2020)
faq.title: Equívocos comuns
faq.subtitle: Os Limites do Crescimento tem sido amplamente citado incorretamente e mal compreendido desde 1972. Aqui estão as objeções mais comuns — e o que o modelo realmente diz.
faq.ran_out.question: "Eles previram que os recursos acabariam até 2000"
faq.ran_out.answer_html: This is the most persistent myth. The Limits to Growth never predicted that any specific resource would run out on a specific date. The model explored scenarios — it showed that exponential growth in consumption, combined with delayed feedback loops, leads to overshoot: a pattern where demand exceeds sustainable supply, followed by decline. The standard run shows resources becoming critically scarce around the 2020s–2030s, not 2000.
faq.technology_saves.question: "A tecnologia vai resolver tudo"
faq.technology_saves.answer_html: The model explicitly tested this. The "optimistic technology" scenario assumes resource efficiency doubles and pollution is halved from 1975 onward. Result: technology delays the peak and raises the ceiling, but does not prevent overshoot on its own. Growth in consumption outpaces the efficiency gains. The model's conclusion isn't anti-technology — it's that technology is necessary but not sufficient without demand-side changes. You can explore this scenario yourself.
faq.too_simple.question: "O modelo é simples demais para ser útil"
faq.too_simple.answer_html: World3 is a systems dynamics model — it captures structural feedback loops, not point predictions. It doesn't try to forecast GDP in 2047 or oil prices in 2030. It identifies the qualitative behavior of a system: does it stabilize, oscillate, or overshoot? This is like how a weather model can tell you "a storm is coming" without predicting exactly which street will flood. Graham Turner's 2008 comparison showed that 30 years of real data tracked the standard run remarkably well — precisely because the model captured the right structural dynamics.
faq.malthus.question: "Malthus estava errado, então isso também deve estar"
faq.malthus.answer_html: Malthus modeled a single exponential (population) hitting a single limit (food). World3 models interconnected feedback loops across five sectors: population, capital, agriculture, resources, and pollution. Overshoot in World3 isn't caused by one resource running out — it's caused by the interaction of multiple stresses: resource depletion diverts capital from industry, which reduces food production, which increases mortality, which eventually reduces population. This is fundamentally different from simple Malthusian arithmetic.
faq.no_collapse_yet.question: "Ainda não entramos em colapso, então o modelo está errado"
faq.no_collapse_yet.answer_html: The standard run shows global population peaking around 2030 and industrial output peaking around 2015, with decline accelerating through the mid-21st century. We are currently at or near the inflection point — not past it. The model never predicted sudden collapse. It predicted a gradual erosion of well-being: declining life expectancy, falling food per capita, rising pollution costs. These trends are consistent with 2020 data (Herrington, 2021). The question isn't whether overshoot has started — it's how societies will respond to it.
intro.book_callout_html: This simulator is based on the World3 model from The Limits to Growth (1972) by Donella Meadows, Dennis Meadows, Jørgen Randers, and William Behrens. If you find these scenarios compelling, read the book — the model is only as meaningful as the assumptions behind it, and the book explains the reasoning in full.
intro.scenario_context_html: The Comprehensive policy scenario is the only one that avoids overshoot. It combines technology improvements with population stabilization and agricultural investment — showing that a sustainable path exists, but requires coordinated action across all sectors.
intro.resources_html:
• The Limits to Growth — Club of Rome

  • Turner (2008) — A comparison of The Limits to Growth with 30 years of reality
  • Herrington (2021) — Update to limits to growth
  • Planetary Boundaries — Stockholm Resilience Centre
  • Breaking Down: Collapse — podcast Ep. 4
intro.ack.item_8_html: Forrester, J. W. — World Dynamics (1971), the precursor model that inspired World3
history.cybernetics.title: The Cybernetics Revolution
history.cybernetics.body_html: Cybernetics — the study of feedback and control in systems, pioneered by Norbert Wiener in the 1940s — showed that systems, whether machines, organisms, or societies, could be understood through their feedback loops: how outputs circle back to influence inputs. In the 1950s and 60s, Jay Forrester at MIT translated these ideas into system dynamics — a method for simulating complex systems on computers. His Industrial Dynamics (1961) and Urban Dynamics (1969) demonstrated that counterintuitive behavior emerges from feedback loops: policies that seem helpful can make problems worse, and delays between cause and effect create oscillations and overshoot. Forrester’s World Dynamics (1971) was the first attempt to simulate the entire global system — population, capital, agriculture, resources, and pollution — as one interconnected model. For the first time, humanity had a tool to ask: what happens when exponential growth meets finite planetary limits? World3 built directly on this foundation.
history.systems_thinking.title: Pensamento sistêmico: O legado de Donella Meadows
history.systems_thinking.body_html: Donella Meadows was not only the lead author of The Limits to Growth — she became one of the most influential systems thinkers of the 20th century. Her 1999 article Leverage Points: Places to Intervene in a System identified twelve places where small changes can produce large effects — from adjusting parameters (weak leverage) to changing the goals and paradigms of a system (powerful leverage). Her posthumous book Thinking in Systems: A Primer (2008) became the standard introduction to systems dynamics, teaching a generation of researchers, policymakers, and activists to see the world in terms of stocks, flows, feedback loops, and delays. Meadows argued that the most important insight from World3 was not any specific prediction — it was the understanding that exponential growth in a finite system produces overshoot not because of any single failure, but because of the structure of the system itself. Changing that structure requires changing how we think.
history.overshoot.title: Overshoot ecológico: Vivendo além dos meios da Terra
history.overshoot.body_html: The central concept of The Limits to Growth is ecological overshoot — the point at which humanity’s demand on nature exceeds what ecosystems can regenerate. Unlike a simple shortage, overshoot is a systemic phenomenon: it happens because feedback signals (declining soil fertility, rising pollution, resource scarcity) arrive with long delays, so growth continues well past the sustainable threshold before consequences become visible. The WWF Living Planet Report and the Global Footprint Network track this empirically: since the early 1970s — precisely the period World3 modeled — humanity has been in overshoot, consuming more biological resources each year than the Earth regenerates. By 2024, Earth Overshoot Day falls in early August, meaning we use roughly 1.7 Earths’ worth of resources annually. The analogy in World3 is the standard run: population and industrial output grow past the carrying capacity, supported temporarily by drawing down nonrenewable resource stocks and degrading the environment. When the accumulated ecological debt becomes too large, the system contracts. This is not a prediction of a single catastrophic event — it is a description of a structural dynamic: growth, overshoot, and decline, driven by delays in the feedback loops that connect human activity to planetary limits.
intro.what_can_you_do_title: O que você pode fazer com este simulador?
intro.what_can_you_do_html: Unlike static explanations, this World3 simulator lets you experience the Limits to Growth model interactively. Run simulations from 1900 to 2100. Explore collapse scenarios, test optimistic technology assumptions, or see how comprehensive policy changes can avoid overshoot. Adjust individual model constants and watch the system respond in real time.
seo.what_is.title: O que é o modelo World3?
seo.what_is.body_html:
World3 is a system dynamics computer model built at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in 1972. It was commissioned by the Club of Rome, an international think tank concerned with the long-term future of humanity and the planet. The model simulates the interactions between five key global variables — population, industrial output, food production, pollution, and nonrenewable natural resources — from 1900 to 2100.

World3 was built on the foundation of Jay Forrester's earlier World Dynamics model (1971). Forrester pioneered system dynamics — a methodology for modeling complex systems using feedback loops, stocks, flows, and time delays. A team led by Dennis Meadows expanded Forrester's work into a much more detailed simulation with five interconnected sectors: population, capital (industry), agriculture, resources, and persistent pollution.

The results were published in the landmark book The Limits to Growth (1972) by Donella Meadows, Dennis Meadows, Jorgen Randers, and William Behrens III. It became one of the best-selling environmental books of all time, with over 30 million copies sold and translations into more than 30 languages.

How World3 Works

The model uses feedback loops to capture how variables influence each other. For example, as industrial output rises, pollution increases, which reduces agricultural yields, which lowers food per capita, which increases mortality, which eventually slows population growth. These interactions create nonlinear dynamics — small changes in one sector can cascade through the entire system.

World3 tracks stocks (quantities that accumulate, like population or resource reserves), flows (rates of change, like birth rates or extraction rates), and delays (the time lag between cause and effect). It is these delays that make overshoot possible: by the time the consequences of overuse become apparent, the system has already moved far past the sustainable threshold.

Not a Prediction — a Scenario Explorer

World3 was never intended to predict the future. It was designed to explore "what if" questions: What happens if population continues growing at current rates? What if technology doubles resource efficiency? What if we combine policy changes across all sectors? The original study examined 13 scenarios, each with different assumptions about technology, policy, and human behavior.

The key insight from World3 is structural: exponential growth on a finite planet leads to overshoot — a condition where demand exceeds the planet's carrying capacity, followed by decline. The model shows that no single technological fix can prevent this; only coordinated changes across population, industry, agriculture, and pollution policies can achieve a sustainable equilibrium.

Graham Turner's 2008 study compared 30 years of real-world data against the original World3 scenarios and found that observed trends closely matched the standard run — lending empirical support to the model's structural accuracy.

Try the World3 simulator to explore these scenarios yourself, or learn more about the history behind the model and how it works mathematically.
seo.ltg.title: Os Limites do Crescimento: o livro que mudou tudo
seo.ltg.body_html:
The Limits to Growth was published in 1972, commissioned by the Club of Rome. It was authored by Donella H. Meadows, Dennis L. Meadows, Jorgen Randers, and William W. Behrens III — a team of researchers at MIT who used the World3 computer model to explore the long-term consequences of continued growth on a finite planet.

Using World3, the team explored 13 scenarios that varied assumptions about technology, resources, population policy, and pollution controls. The central finding was stark: under business-as-usual assumptions (the "standard run"), population and industrial output would overshoot the planet's carrying capacity by the mid-21st century, followed by an unavoidable decline in both.

Not a Prediction of Doom

Contrary to popular mischaracterization, The Limits to Growth was never a prediction that civilization would collapse by a specific date. It was a call to change course. The book explicitly showed that sustainable pathways were possible — but only if humanity acted early enough and across multiple fronts simultaneously: technology, population stabilization, pollution reduction, and resource conservation.

The authors wrote: "If the present growth trends in world population, industrialization, pollution, food production, and resource depletion continue unchanged, the limits to growth on this planet will be reached sometime within the next one hundred years. The most probable result will be a rather sudden and uncontrollable decline in both population and industrial capacity."

Backlash and Misrepresentation

The book was widely attacked after publication. Critics — many of them economists — accused the authors of ignoring the power of markets and technology to solve resource constraints. The most common straw man was that the book "predicted the world would run out of oil by 1992," which it never did. The model explored scenarios; it did not make point predictions about specific resources.

Much of the criticism stemmed from disciplinary bias: economists trained in equilibrium models found it difficult to accept a systems dynamics model that showed structural overshoot as an emergent property of feedback loops and delays.

Vindication: Graham Turner's 2008 Study

In 2008, physicist Graham Turner at CSIRO compared 30 years of actual data (1972-2002) against the original World3 scenarios. His conclusion: the real world tracked the standard run with remarkable fidelity. Population, food production, industrial output, and pollution all followed the model's business-as-usual trajectory. This was a powerful vindication of the model's structural accuracy.

The 30-Year Update and Beyond

In 2005, three of the original authors published Limits to Growth: The 30-Year Update, revisiting the model with updated data. Their conclusion was sobering: the window for a smooth transition to sustainability had narrowed significantly since 1972. Many of the early intervention opportunities had been missed.

The Limits to Growth also anticipated what scientists would later formalize as planetary boundaries — the nine biophysical thresholds that define a safe operating space for humanity. Six of these nine boundaries have now been crossed.

With over 30 million copies sold and translations into more than 30 languages, The Limits to Growth remains one of the most important environmental texts ever published. Visit our FAQ for common misconceptions, or explore the scenarios yourself in the interactive simulator.
seo.scenarios.title: Cenários do World3: colapso, tecnologia e caminhos sustentáveis
seo.scenarios.body_html:
The World3 model produced 13 scenarios in the original Limits to Growth study, each exploring different assumptions about technology, policy, resources, and human behavior. This simulator features five key scenarios that illustrate the range of possible outcomes — from overshoot and decline to sustainable equilibrium.

1. Standard Run: Business as Usual

The standard run assumes no major changes in physical, economic, or social relationships that have historically governed the world system. Population and industrial output grow exponentially until they encounter resource limits. Nonrenewable resources begin to deplete around 2000-2010, diverting capital from productive investment to resource extraction. Industrial output peaks around 2015, food production per capita begins declining around 2020, and population peaks around 2030 at roughly 8.4 billion before declining as life expectancy falls.

This is not a prediction of sudden catastrophe — it is a description of gradual systemic decline driven by the interaction of resource depletion, rising pollution costs, and falling investment in food and services.

2. Optimistic Technology

The optimistic technology scenario assumes resource efficiency doubles and pollution per unit of output is halved from 1975 onward. The result: technology delays the peak and raises the ceiling, but does not prevent overshoot. Growth in consumption outpaces efficiency gains, and the system still enters decline — just later and from a higher peak. The lesson: technology is necessary but not sufficient.

3. Doubled Resources

The doubled resources scenario starts with twice the initial stock of nonrenewable resources. This buys time — industrial output peaks later — but pollution accumulates more severely because the economy runs hotter for longer. The eventual decline is driven by pollution damage to agriculture and human health rather than resource scarcity. More resources without pollution controls simply shifts which limit triggers the overshoot.

4. Population Stability

The population stability scenario assumes earlier family size reduction (desired family size drops to two children sooner). This helps: population peaks lower, and per-capita resource availability improves. But it is not sufficient on its own — industrial output still overshoots the resource base, and pollution continues to accumulate. Population policy alone cannot solve a systemic problem.

5. Comprehensive Policy

The comprehensive policy scenario is the only one that avoids overshoot. It combines technology improvements (resource efficiency, pollution controls) with population stabilization (earlier family planning) and agricultural investment (land yield enhancement with erosion prevention). Under these coordinated policies, population stabilizes around 8 billion, industrial output levels off at a comfortable standard of living, food per capita remains adequate, and pollution stays within manageable bounds.

The key lesson from the comprehensive policy scenario: no single intervention works. Technology alone fails. Population policy alone fails. Resource abundance alone fails. Only coordinated change across all sectors can achieve a sustainable outcome.

What These Scenarios Mean

The World3 scenarios are not forecasts — they are explorations of structural dynamics. They show that the behavior of the system (growth, overshoot, decline, or stabilization) depends on the structure of the feedbacks, not on any single variable. The scenarios demonstrate that humanity has choices, but those choices must be systemic and timely.

Explore all scenarios in the simulator to see the dynamics for yourself. See our FAQ for common misconceptions about the model, or read the history of how these ideas developed.
preset.recalibration-2023.name: Recalibração 2023
preset.recalibration-2023.description: Recalibração de Nebel et al. 2023 — constantes ajustadas a dados empíricos de 1970–2020.
compare.diverge_warning: Divergence has no effect for this pair — their differences are initial conditions set at 1900, not policy changes.
intro.ai_prompt_desc: Copie qualquer um dos prompts abaixo no ChatGPT, Claude ou qualquer assistente de IA. A IA pode chamar a API do World3 em limits.world para executar simulações e responder suas perguntas.
intro.ai_prompt_1_label: E se as taxas de natalidade caíssem pela metade?
intro.ai_prompt_1: Use a API do simulador World3 em https://limits.world (veja /llm.txt para instruções). O que aconteceria com a população mundial, expectativa de vida e recursos se as taxas de natalidade globais caíssem pela metade a partir de 2026? Compare com o cenário padrão.
intro.ai_prompt_2_label: E se a eficiência dos recursos dobrasse?
intro.ai_prompt_2: Use a API do simulador World3 em https://limits.world (veja /llm.txt para instruções). O que aconteceria se a eficiência de extração de recursos dobrasse e a poluição fosse reduzida pela metade com tecnologia, a partir de 2026? Mostre as tendências de população, produção industrial e poluição comparadas ao cenário padrão.
intro.ai_prompt_3: Use a API do simulador World3 em https://limits.world (veja /llm.txt para instruções). Quero explorar um cenário personalizado: [descreva sua pergunta aqui]. Execute a simulação e explique o que acontece com a civilização até 2100.
